bytes.topic: IaC Philosophy — reproducibility, drift detection, auditability, why IaC matters
bytes.title: Ansible IaC Philosophy — Reproducibility, Drift, and Why Idempotency Is the Point
bytes.body:
Ansible's IaC value isn't automation speed — it's guaranteed state. Every task in a playbook is idempotent by contract: `ansible.builtin.user`, `ansible.builtin.copy`, `ansible.builtin.systemd` only change what differs from the declared state. Run the same playbook 10 times; the 2nd through 10th runs are no-ops if nothing drifted.

Drift detection is built into `--check` mode. Combined with `--diff`, you get a line-level delta of what the live system diverged from your source-of-truth inventory and roles — without applying anything. Pair this with a scheduled CI job (`ansible-playbook --check`) and you have continuous compliance auditing.

Auditability comes from the playbook itself: tasks are declarative prose, not imperative scripts. `changed_when` and `failed_when` make outcomes explicit. Every role in `requirements.yml` is pinned by version, so a `galaxy install` from six months ago produces the same role tree today.

The key discipline: never SSH and fix manually. Every remediation goes through the playbook. If the playbook can't express it, that's a gap to close in your roles — not a one-off exception.
bytes.code_snippet:
# scheduled drift detection — runs in CI without applying changes
- name: Detect configuration drift
  hosts: production
  gather_facts: true

  roles:
    - role: hardening
    - role: app_config

# CI invocation (GitHub Actions, etc.)
# ansible-playbook site.yml --check --diff \
#   -i inventories/production/hosts.yml \
#   --vault-password-file .vault_pass
#
# Exit code 0 = no drift. Non-zero = drift found → alert or open ticket.

bytes.topic: State Management — remote state, state locking, backend configuration
bytes.title: Ansible Fact Caching — Remote State, Redis Backends, and Locking Gaps
bytes.body:
Ansible doesn't persist state like Terraform, but fact caching fills the gap. By configuring a caching backend in `ansible.cfg`, gathered facts survive across playbook runs — enabling cross-playbook fact sharing and cutting SSH round-trips on large inventories.

Redis is the standard backend for distributed teams. Set `fact_caching = redis`, `fact_caching_connection = redis://localhost:6379/0`, and `fact_caching_timeout = 86400` under `[defaults]`. With `gathering = smart`, Ansible skips collection entirely when cached data is fresh — a measurable speed-up on inventories with hundreds of hosts.

Ansible has no native state locking. Concurrent playbook runs against the same inventory risk cache corruption or task interleaving. AWX/Ansible Automation Platform handles this through its built-in job scheduler and resource locking. For standalone Ansible, wrap invocations with `flock -n /tmp/ansible.lock ansible-playbook site.yml` or set `serial: 1` at the play level to cap parallelism to one host batch at a time.

Inspect cached facts with `ansible -m cache -a 'dump'` or query Redis directly via `redis-cli`. Flush stale entries mid-play using `meta: clear_facts`, which resets the in-memory cache for the current play without touching the persistent backend.
bytes.code_snippet:
# ansible.cfg — configure remote fact cache backend
[defaults]
gathering             = smart
fact_caching          = redis
fact_caching_connection = redis://localhost:6379/0
fact_caching_timeout  = 86400

[inventory]
cache             = True
cache_plugin      = jsonfile
cache_connection  = /tmp/ansible_inventory_cache
cache_timeout     = 3600

# External lock prevents concurrent playbook runs from corrupting cache
flock -n /tmp/ansible.lock \
  ansible-playbook site.yml -i inventory/prod

bytes.topic: Modules — reusable modules, registry, versioning strategy
bytes.title: Ansible Collections — Reusable Modules, Galaxy Registry, and Versioning Strategy
bytes.body: Ansible Collections (introduced in 2.9, mandatory post-2.10) are the canonical unit for distributing reusable modules, roles, and plugins. A collection lives under `collections/ansible_collections/<namespace>/<name>/` and is declared in `galaxy.yml` with a semantic version. Publish to Ansible Galaxy or a private Automation Hub using `ansible-galaxy collection build` then `ansible-galaxy collection publish`. Pin dependencies in `requirements.yml` using exact (`==1.4.2`) or constrained (`>=1.4,<2.0`) version ranges — never omit the version pin in production, since Galaxy collections are mutable by default. For air-gapped environments, build a `.tar.gz` artifact in CI and install offline with `ansible-galaxy collection install ./ns-name-1.4.2.tar.gz`. Inside your collection, prefix custom modules under `plugins/modules/` and document them with `DOCUMENTATION`, `EXAMPLES`, and `RETURN` YAML blocks so `ansible-doc` works out of the box. Use `meta/runtime.yml` to declare the minimum `requires_ansible` version — this prevents silent breakage when consumers run older Ansible cores.
bytes.code_snippet:
# requirements.yml — pin collections for reproducible installs
collections:
  - name: community.general
    version: ">=8.0.0,<9.0.0"
  - name: amazon.aws
    version: "==7.4.0"
  - name: myorg.platform
    source: https://automation-hub.internal/api/galaxy/
    version: "==2.1.3"

# Install pinned collections
# ansible-galaxy collection install -r requirements.yml -p ./collections

# Build and publish your own collection
# ansible-galaxy collection build collections/ansible_collections/myorg/platform
# ansible-galaxy collection publish myorg-platform-2.1.3.tar.gz --server automation_hub

bytes.topic: Workspaces & Environments — managing dev/staging/prod with the same codebase
bytes.title: Ansible Multi-Environment Inventories — One Codebase, Three Environments
bytes.body:
Ansible handles dev/staging/prod separation through directory-scoped inventories paired with group_vars. Instead of a flat hosts file, structure each environment as its own inventory directory containing hosts.yml and a group_vars/ subtree. Your playbooks stay untouched — only the -i flag changes at runtime.

Define shared defaults in group_vars/all/vars.yml, then override per-environment in group_vars/webservers/vars.yml within each inventory directory. Variable precedence resolves environment-specific values at play time without any conditionals in your roles.

For secrets, pair each environment's group_vars with an ansible-vault encrypted vault.yml file. Use a per-environment vault password file (.vault_pass_prod, .vault_pass_staging) referenced via --vault-password-file, never hardcoded. Add all vault password files to .gitignore.

To enforce environment promotion hygiene, add a pre_tasks block that asserts ansible_limit is set, preventing accidental all-host runs against prod. Combine this with tagged plays (--tags deploy) to gate destructive operations behind explicit intent rather than relying on operator discipline.
bytes.code_snippet:
inventories/
  dev/
    hosts.yml
    group_vars/
      all/vars.yml        # db_port: 5432, env: dev
      webservers/vars.yml # replicas: 1
  prod/
    hosts.yml
    group_vars/
      all/vars.yml        # db_port: 5432, env: prod
      all/vault.yml       # db_password: !vault |
      webservers/vars.yml # replicas: 4

# Run against prod only:
# ansible-playbook -i inventories/prod site.yml --vault-password-file .vault_pass_prod

bytes.topic: Bicep — ARM template replacement, Azure-native, modules and parameters
bytes.title: Ansible + Bicep — Deploy Azure-Native IaC Without Leaving Your Playbooks
bytes.body:
If you manage Azure resources with `azure.azcollection`, raw ARM JSON is a maintenance burden. Bicep compiles down to ARM JSON but reads like a real language — think of it as Jinja2 templating applied to Azure resource declarations.

Bicep modules map directly to Ansible roles: each `.bicep` file encapsulates a reusable resource unit (VNets, AKS clusters, storage accounts). Parameters replace `vars_files` — declare them at the top with explicit types and defaults, then override at deploy time, identical to `--extra-vars` semantics.

From your playbooks, invoke Bicep via `ansible.builtin.command` with `az deployment group create`. Capture outputs using `register`, then pipe resource IDs (connection strings, endpoint URLs) into downstream tasks with `set_fact`. Alternatively, compile Bicep to ARM JSON with `az bicep build` and pass the result directly to `azure.azcollection.azure_rm_deployment` for native module integration and idempotency tracking.

The division of responsibility: Bicep owns *what* Azure resources exist and their configuration; Ansible owns *orchestration* — sequencing deployments, gating on `azure_rm_deployment`'s `wait` flag, and threading outputs across your play.
bytes.code_snippet:
- name: Deploy storage module via Bicep
  ansible.builtin.command:
    cmd: >
      az deployment group create
      --resource-group {{ resource_group }}
      --template-file modules/storage.bicep
      --parameters storageAccountName={{ storage_name }}
                   skuName=Standard_LRS
                   location={{ azure_location }}
  register: bicep_result

- name: Extract storage endpoint from Bicep output
  ansible.builtin.set_fact:
    storage_endpoint: "{{ (bicep_result.stdout | from_json).properties.outputs.blobEndpoint.value }}"

bytes.topic: Pulumi — real programming languages for IaC, type safety, testing support
bytes.title: Ansible vs Pulumi — Why Real Languages Beat YAML for Complex IaC
bytes.body:
Ansible's YAML playbooks hit a ceiling fast: Jinja2 templating for logic, untyped `register` variables, and Molecule for integration-only testing. Pulumi replaces the YAML layer with real Python, TypeScript, or Go — the same languages Ansible modules are written in.

Where Ansible forces `"{{ hostvars[inventory_hostname]['ansible_default_ipv4']['address'] }}"`, Pulumi gives you a strongly-typed `Output[str]` with full IDE autocomplete. Resource arguments are dataclasses — a typo in a property name is caught at author-time, not mid-run with a cryptic task failure.

Testing is the sharpest difference. Ansible's Molecule spins up real VMs or containers to validate playbooks. Pulumi's SDK exposes a `mock` interface: pass fake provider responses and assert resource configs with pytest or Jest — no AWS account, no containers, no 4-minute feedback loop. This enables true unit tests for infra logic and makes CI 10x faster.

Ansible remains superior for configuration management and idempotent SSH-based provisioning. Pulumi targets cloud resource provisioning — the layer above Ansible. Most mature teams run both: Pulumi provisions the cloud infra, Ansible configures what runs on it.
bytes.code_snippet:
import pulumi
import pulumi_aws as aws

# Typos caught by IDE/mypy — not at runtime like Ansible task failures
bucket = aws.s3.Bucket("app-bucket",
    versioning=aws.s3.BucketVersioningArgs(enabled=True),
    tags={"Env": "prod", "ManagedBy": "pulumi"}
)

# Strongly-typed Output[str] — no more dict-drilling like Ansible register vars
pulumi.export("bucket_id", bucket.id)

# In test_infra.py (pytest, no live AWS):
# with pulumi.runtime.set_mocks(MyMocks()):
#     assert stack.outputs["bucket_id"].apply(lambda v: v.startswith("app-"))

bytes.topic: Ansible — agentless config management, playbooks, idempotency
bytes.title: Ansible Idempotency — Write Playbooks That Are Safe to Run Twice
bytes.body: Ansible pushes configuration over SSH without agents on target hosts — the control node connects, executes modules, and disconnects. The contract is idempotency: running a playbook ten times must produce identical state to running it once. Most built-in modules honor this automatically. `ansible.builtin.package` checks installed state before touching the package manager. `ansible.builtin.copy` hashes file content and skips the transfer if unchanged. `ansible.builtin.user` reads `/etc/passwd` before creating accounts. Where idempotency breaks down is `ansible.builtin.command` and `ansible.builtin.shell` — these run unconditionally unless guarded with `creates`, `removes`, or a `when` conditional tied to a registered variable. Prefer purpose-built modules over shell commands wherever possible; they carry the idempotency guarantee built in. For truly stateful operations (database migrations, one-time bootstraps), use `run_once: true` combined with a sentinel file checked via `ansible.builtin.stat` to prevent re-execution. Structure playbooks as declarations of desired state, not imperative scripts, and Ansible's check mode (`--check`) becomes a free dry-run across your entire fleet.
bytes.code_snippet:
- name: Ensure nginx config is deployed
  hosts: webservers
  become: true
  tasks:
    - name: Install nginx
      ansible.builtin.package:
        name: nginx
        state: present

    - name: Deploy config template
      ansible.builtin.template:
        src: nginx.conf.j2
        dest: /etc/nginx/nginx.conf
        owner: root
        mode: '0644'
      notify: Reload nginx

    - name: Run migration only once
      ansible.builtin.command: /opt/app/migrate.sh
      args:
        creates: /opt/app/.migrated

  handlers:
    - name: Reload nginx
      ansible.builtin.service:
        name: nginx
        state: reloaded

bytes.topic: AWS CDK — constructs, stacks, L1/L2/L3 abstraction levels
bytes.title: Ansible to AWS CDK — L1/L2/L3 Constructs Through an Ansible Lens
bytes.body:
Coming from Ansible, CDK's abstraction model maps cleanly to what you already know. A Stack is your playbook — a unit of deployment that groups related resources and deploys atomically. Constructs are your roles — composable, reusable building blocks you scope and nest.

CDK exposes three abstraction levels. L1 constructs (Cfn* prefix, e.g. CfnBucket) are raw CloudFormation resources — equivalent to Ansible's cloudformation module with full resource YAML. Every property is manual; no defaults. L2 constructs (e.g. Bucket) are the sweet spot: opinionated wrappers with sane defaults, like a well-maintained community.aws role that handles IAM grants, encryption, and access logging without you specifying each. L3 constructs (patterns) are full solutions — think ApplicationLoadBalancedFargateService — comparable to a curated Ansible playbook that wires up an entire production topology in one call.

Stay at L2 for most resources. Drop to L1 only when a CloudFormation property isn't surfaced by the L2 API. Unlike Ansible's declarative YAML, CDK is imperative Python or TypeScript — you can loop, branch, and compose constructs dynamically, which replaces much of what you'd do with Jinja2 templating or include_tasks.
bytes.code_snippet:
from aws_cdk import Stack, aws_s3 as s3
from aws_cdk.aws_s3 import CfnBucket
from constructs import Construct

class StorageStack(Stack):
    def __init__(self, scope: Construct, id: str, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(scope, id, **kwargs)

        # L1 — raw CFN, like Ansible's cloudformation module
        CfnBucket(self, "RawBucket",
            versioning_configuration=CfnBucket.VersioningConfigurationProperty(status="Enabled"))

        # L2 — opinionated defaults, like a community.aws role
        s3.Bucket(self, "ManagedBucket",
            versioned=True,
            encryption=s3.BucketEncryption.S3_MANAGED,
            block_public_access=s3.BlockPublicAccess.BLOCK_ALL)

bytes.topic: Secrets in IaC — never store secrets in state, vault/secrets manager integration
bytes.title: Ansible Secrets — Vault Integration and Why Plaintext Vars Will Burn You
bytes.body:
Storing secrets in `group_vars/` or `host_vars/` as plaintext is the fastest way to leak credentials through version control. Ansible gives you two clean paths out.

First: `ansible-vault encrypt_string` wraps individual values inline — no separate vault file needed. Mark encrypted strings with `!vault` and Ansible decrypts them at runtime using `--vault-password-file` or `ANSIBLE_VAULT_PASSWORD_FILE`.

Second, and preferred for production: pull secrets from HashiCorp Vault or AWS Secrets Manager using `community.hashi_vault.hashi_vault` or `amazon.aws.aws_ssm` lookup plugins. Secrets are fetched at play runtime, never written to disk, and never appear in `ansible --diff` output or log files.

Critical: set `no_log: true` on any task that registers or uses a secret variable. Without it, Ansible prints variable values on failure. Also disable `ANSIBLE_DEBUG` in CI — it dumps all variable values. Use `ansible-lint` with the `no-log-password` rule to enforce this in pipelines. Your state — inventory, vars, facts cache — should contain zero secret material.
bytes.code_snippet:
- name: Fetch DB password from HashiCorp Vault
  hosts: app_servers
  vars:
    db_password: "{{ lookup('community.hashi_vault.hashi_vault',
      'secret=secret/data/prod/db:password
      url=https://vault.internal:8200
      auth_method=aws_iam
      role_id=prod-app') }}"
  tasks:
    - name: Configure database connection
      ansible.builtin.template:
        src: db.conf.j2
        dest: /etc/app/db.conf
        mode: '0600'
      no_log: true

bytes.topic: Testing IaC — terratest, checkov, tfsec, policy as code
bytes.title: Ansible IaC Testing — Molecule, Checkov, and ansible-lint as Policy Gates
bytes.body: Ansible roles aren't exempt from IaC quality gates. Three tools cover the full testing pyramid. Molecule handles integration testing: it spins up ephemeral Docker or Podman containers, converges your role, then runs `verify.yml` assertions using the `ansible.builtin.assert` module against actual system state — not mocked state. For static security analysis, Checkov natively scans Ansible playbooks and flags issues like `become: yes` without explicit `become_user`, world-writable file modes, or hardcoded secrets in `vars:` blocks. Run it in CI with `checkov -d . --framework ansible --compact`. ansible-lint fills the policy-as-code role: write custom rules in Python under `rules/` that enforce org-wide conventions — required tags, forbidden modules, naming patterns. Set `warn_list` vs `error_list` in `.ansible-lint` to control gate severity. The enforcement hierarchy is: lint (fast, pre-commit) → checkov (security, PR gate) → Molecule (integration, merge gate). Skipping Molecule on role changes that touch `handlers/` or `defaults/` is a common source of silent regressions.
bytes.code_snippet:
# .ansible-lint
profile: production
error_list:
  - yaml[truthy]
  - no-free-form
  - risky-file-permissions
warn_list:
  - experimental

# rules/RequireTagsRule.py
from ansiblelint.rules import AnsibleLintRule

class RequireTagsRule(AnsibleLintRule):
    id = "LOCAL001"
    description = "All tasks must define at least one tag"
    severity = "HIGH"

    def matchtask(self, task, file=None):
        return not task.get("tags")

bytes.topic: Import Existing Resources — bringing existing infra under IaC management
bytes.title: Ansible Import Existing Resources — Bringing Live Infra Under IaC Control
bytes.body:
When infra was provisioned manually before Ansible existed in your workflow, you need to bring it under management without destroying and recreating it. The key is `gather_facts` combined with idempotent modules — Ansible doesn't track state like Terraform, so "importing" means writing tasks that describe desired state and ensuring they don't cause drift on first run.

For most resources, the workflow is: run the relevant module with `--check --diff` first to see what Ansible *would* change. If the diff is empty, the resource is already compliant. For packages, use `ansible.builtin.package` with `state: present`. For files, use `ansible.builtin.copy` or `ansible.builtin.template` with `force: false` on first pass to avoid overwriting live configs.

For cloud resources (EC2, S3), use `amazon.aws` collection modules — they're idempotent by design and will reconcile existing resources via resource identifiers like `instance_ids` or `bucket_name`. Use `register` + `debug` to inspect what was found vs. changed. The golden rule: describe what *should* be true, not what *to do* — Ansible handles the rest.
bytes.code_snippet:
- name: Reconcile existing EC2 instance under Ansible management
  amazon.aws.ec2_instance:
    instance_ids:
      - i-0abc123def456789
    state: running
    tags:
      ManagedBy: ansible
      Env: production
  register: ec2_result

- name: Ensure nginx is present (idempotent on existing installs)
  ansible.builtin.package:
    name: nginx
    state: present

- name: Lay down config without overwriting live file on first run
  ansible.builtin.copy:
    src: nginx.conf
    dest: /etc/nginx/nginx.conf
    force: false
    owner: root
    mode: '0644'

bytes.topic: Destroy Safety — preventing accidental destruction, lifecycle rules
bytes.title: Ansible Destroy Safety — Preventing Accidental Resource Deletion with Lifecycle Guards
bytes.body: Ansible has no native `prevent_destroy` like Terraform, so destruction safety must be built deliberately. The first line of defense is `--check` mode combined with `--diff`, which dry-runs playbooks and shows what would change without applying it. For critical infrastructure, gate destructive tasks behind a `when` condition tied to an explicit variable: `when: allow_destroy | default(false)`. This forces callers to pass `-e allow_destroy=true` consciously, making accidents loud. Use `block` with `rescue` to catch failures on deletion tasks and emit alerts before re-raising. For cloud resources managed via `amazon.aws` or `azure.azcollection`, set `termination_protection: true` on the resource itself — Ansible will respect the provider's API rejection. Pair this with `tags` filtering: mark destructive plays with `tags: [destructive]` and exclude them in CI pipelines using `--skip-tags destructive`. Finally, use `assert` tasks at playbook entry to validate environment name, preventing prod teardowns triggered from wrong inventory groups.
bytes.code_snippet:
- name: Guard destructive teardown
  hosts: all
  vars:
    allow_destroy: false
  tasks:
    - name: Abort if destroy not explicitly enabled
      assert:
        that:
          - allow_destroy | bool
          - target_env != 'production'
        fail_msg: "Destroy blocked. Pass -e allow_destroy=true and target non-prod."
      tags: [destructive]

    - name: Terminate EC2 instance
      amazon.aws.ec2_instance:
        instance_ids: "{{ instance_id }}"
        state: absent
      when: allow_destroy | bool
      tags: [destructive]

bytes.topic: Cost Estimation — infracost, estimating before apply
bytes.title: Ansible Cost Gates — Blocking Deploys with Infracost Before Apply
bytes.body:
Infracost doesn't parse Ansible's imperative resource tasks — it targets Terraform HCL. But Ansible is often the orchestration layer that *calls* Terraform, making it the right place to enforce cost gates. Use `ansible.builtin.command` to invoke `infracost breakdown --format json` against your Terraform directory, `register` stdout, then pipe through `from_json` to extract `totalMonthlyCost`. A downstream `ansible.builtin.assert` task aborts the entire play if projected spend exceeds a threshold — zero cloud resources touched, zero surprise bills.

Define `cost_threshold` in `group_vars/all.yml` so limits are environment-scoped: `$50` for dev, `$500` for prod. Drop the check into a `pre_tasks` block in your provisioning playbook so it runs before any `community.aws.*` or `azure.azcollection.*` tasks fire. For change detection, run `infracost diff --compare-to baseline.json` and assert the monthly delta is within an acceptable range before allowing scale-up plays to proceed. This turns Ansible from a blind executor into a cost-aware provisioning controller.
bytes.code_snippet:
- name: Estimate infrastructure cost
  ansible.builtin.command:
    cmd: infracost breakdown --path ./terraform --format json
    chdir: "{{ playbook_dir }}"
  register: infracost_result
  changed_when: false

- name: Extract monthly cost
  ansible.builtin.set_fact:
    monthly_cost: "{{ (infracost_result.stdout | from_json).totalMonthlyCost | float }}"

- name: Gate on cost threshold
  ansible.builtin.assert:
    that:
      - monthly_cost <= cost_threshold | float
    fail_msg: >-
      Estimated ${{ monthly_cost }}/mo exceeds threshold ${{ cost_threshold }}/mo.
      Aborting provisioning play.
  vars:
    cost_threshold: "{{ env_cost_limit | default(200.0) }}"

bytes.topic: CI/CD Integration — automated plan on PR, apply on merge, drift alerts
bytes.title: Ansible CI/CD — Automated Check on PR, Apply on Merge, Nightly Drift Alerts
bytes.body:
Ansible's `--check` mode is your CI plan gate — it dry-runs plays without mutating state, surfacing `changed` and `failed` tasks before anything touches prod. Wire it to PR checks alongside `ansible-lint` for policy enforcement.

On PR: run `ansible-playbook site.yml -i inventory/prod --check --diff`. The `--diff` flag shows exact Jinja2 template and file deltas inline, giving reviewers real signal. Fail the pipeline on any non-zero exit code.

On merge to main: drop `--check` and execute the full apply. Use `--limit` to target only affected host groups and `--tags` to scope execution when your playbook spans many roles.

For drift detection, schedule a nightly `--check` run against prod inventory. If the play exits with `changed` tasks, a downstream step fires a Slack alert via `community.general.slack`. This catches out-of-band manual changes, OS package updates, or config rot before they cause incidents.

Store Vault secrets in GitHub Actions secrets; decrypt at runtime with `--vault-password-file` pointing to a tempfile written from `${{ secrets.VAULT_PASS }}`. Never pass the password as a CLI argument — it leaks into process listings.
bytes.code_snippet:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Invoked from GitHub Actions — GITHUB_EVENT_NAME injected by runner
set -euo pipefail

echo "$VAULT_PASS" > /tmp/.vault_pass
ansible-lint site.yml

FLAGS="-i inventory/prod --vault-password-file /tmp/.vault_pass"

if [[ "$GITHUB_EVENT_NAME" == "pull_request" || "$GITHUB_EVENT_NAME" == "schedule" ]]; then
  ansible-playbook site.yml $FLAGS --check --diff
else
  ansible-playbook site.yml $FLAGS
fi

# schedule run: non-zero changed= exit triggers downstream Slack notify step

bytes.topic: Linting & Formatting — tflint, terraform fmt, consistent style
bytes.title: Ansible Linting — ansible-lint and yamllint for Consistent Playbook Style
bytes.body:
Ansible has no built-in formatter, but combining ansible-lint with yamllint gives you enforced consistency across playbooks, roles, and collections. ansible-lint checks for anti-patterns: using command/shell instead of purpose-built modules, missing name attributes on tasks, incorrect handler notifications, and FQCN violations (e.g. copy instead of ansible.builtin.copy). yamllint handles the YAML layer — trailing spaces, indentation width, line length, and quote style.

Define both configs at repo root. In .ansible-lint, set warn_list vs error on rule severity, skip_list for intentional bypasses, and profile: production to catch the strictest violations. In .yamllint, pin indent.spaces: 2, line-length.max: 160, and truthy.allowed-values: [true, false] to prevent yes/no/on/off surprises.

Run both in pre-commit hooks and CI. ansible-lint --fix auto-remediates many violations (FQCN rewrites, missing names). Pair with molecule lint to catch role-level issues before they hit staging. Enforcing yamllint+ansible-lint as blocking CI gates eliminates entire classes of subtle bugs caused by inconsistent variable quoting and module misuse.
bytes.code_snippet:
# .ansible-lint
profile: production
warn_list:
  - yaml[line-length]
skip_list:
  - role-name  # monorepo layout exception

# .yamllint
---
extends: default
rules:
  indentation:
    spaces: 2
  line-length:
    max: 160
  truthy:
    allowed-values: ["true", "false"]

# pre-commit config
- repo: https://github.com/ansible/ansible-lint
  rev: v24.2.0
  hooks:
    - id: ansible-lint
